sections.heading: BARMM remains highest, but its lead is smaller
sections.body: BARMM still had the highest regional total fertility rate in 2025, at 2.4 births per woman, but this was down from 3.1 in 2022. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ was next at 2.3, and Caraga was also near the top at 2.2. These regional rates are still much higher than the national rate of 1.7, but the highest rates came down between 2022 and 2025. The change was clearest in BARMM, where fertility fell by 0.7 births per woman, more than the national decline of 0.2.

sections.heading: Lower fertility is not only in the capital
sections.body: CALABARZON had the lowest regional fertility rate in 2025, at 1.3 births per woman. NCR and the Negros Island Region were also low at 1.4, while Davao Region was at 1.6. All of these were below the national rate of 1.7, showing that low fertility is not limited to the capital. The pattern shows that several regions, not only NCR, had fertility rates below the national level.

sections.heading: Some regions barely changed, while others dropped sharply
sections.body: The national total fertility rate fell from 1.9 in 2022 to 1.7 in 2025, but regions did not move in the same way. Eastern Visayas stayed at 2.0, and Central Luzon stayed at 1.9. In contrast, SOCCSKSARGEN fell from 2.5 to 1.9, BARMM from 3.1 to 2.4, and CALABARZON from 1.8 to 1.3. The regional map therefore shows both steady levels and recent drops, rather than one uniform national decline.

sections.heading: Regional differences are still wide
sections.body: In 2025, regional fertility ranged from 1.3 births per woman in CALABARZON to 2.4 in BARMM. That is a spread of 1.1 births per woman, even though many regions were grouped between 1.7 and 2.0. The middle includes Cagayan Valley, CAR, and Central Visayas at 1.7, Ilocos Region at 1.8, and several regions at 1.9. This spread matters because the national average hides very different fertility situations across regions.

sections.heading: Most childbearing is in the late twenties and early thirties
sections.body: In 2025, age-specific fertility rates were highest among women ages 25 to 29, at 94 births per 1,000 women. The next highest rate was for ages 30 to 34 at 84, followed by ages 20 to 24 at 67. This means childbearing is centered in the late twenties and early thirties, not the teenage years. The rate for ages 15 to 19 was much lower at 22, and rates after age 40 were also low.

sections.heading: Rural fertility is higher, but both rural and urban rates fell
sections.body: Rural women had a total fertility rate of 2.0 in 2025, compared with 1.5 among urban women. Both groups fell by the same amount from 2022, when rural fertility was 2.2 and urban fertility was 1.7. Because both declined together, the rural-urban gap stayed at 0.5 births per woman. This was not a case where one group changed and the other did not; both moved down from different starting points.

sections.heading: Different fertility measures all show a decline
sections.body: The total fertility rate, general fertility rate, and crude birth rate all went down from 2022 to 2025. The general fertility rate fell from 63 to 57 births per 1,000 women aged 15 to 44, while the crude birth rate declined from 13.3 to 11.6 births per 1,000 population. Urban and rural values also went down on both measures. This matters because the decline appears whether fertility is measured per woman, per women of reproductive age, or per total population.

sections.heading: Fertility generally falls with more schooling, but not in a straight line
sections.body: Women with Grades 1 to 6 education had the highest fertility rate in 2025, at 3.1 births per woman. Fertility was lower among women with no education at 2.3 and among women with Grades 7 to 10 at 2.3. It then fell to 1.8 for Grades 11 to 12, 1.6 for post-secondary, and 1.3 for college. Overall, fertility is much lower at higher levels of schooling, but the highest rate is not in the no-education group. So the pattern is more specific than saying fertility drops at every single step of education.

sections.heading: Wealth shows one of the clearest fertility gaps
sections.body: In 2025, the total fertility rate was 2.8 births per woman in the lowest wealth quintile and 1.1 in the highest quintile. The rate fell at every step across the wealth distribution: 2.8, 2.1, 1.7, 1.3, and 1.1. The gap between the lowest and highest quintiles was 1.7 births per woman, wider than the rural-urban gap. This makes wealth one of the sharpest social divides in the fertility profile.

sections.heading: The national drop is small, but it appears across many groups
sections.body: The national total fertility rate declined from 1.9 births per woman in 2022 to 1.7 in 2025. This change is smaller than the biggest regional and subgroup changes, but it is supported by declines by residence, several age-specific rates, summary measures, and wealth groups. The national figure is therefore more than a standalone average. It reflects a broader pattern in which many parts of the fertility profile moved downward at the same time.

takeaways: The national total fertility rate fell from 1.9 births per woman in 2022 to 1.7 in 2025.
Regional fertility still varies widely, from 1.3 in CALABARZON to 2.4 in BARMM.
Childbearing is concentrated among women ages 25 to 34, with the highest age-specific rate at ages 25 to 29.
Wealth shows a sharper fertility gap than residence: 2.8 births per woman in the lowest quintile compared with 1.1 in the highest.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
